TSMC Delivers Strong 2Q26 Results as 2nm Process Contributes Revenue for the First Time  TSMC reported strong results for 2Q26, driven by robust demand from the HPC market. Quarterly revenue reached $40.2B, up 33.7% YoY, while gross margin climbed to 67.7%. Net income surged 77.4% YoY, setting a new company record.  Advanced Nodes Approach 80% of Revenue TSMC announced that its 2nm process contributed wafer revenue for the first time in 2Q26, accounting for 3% of total wafer revenue. Combined with 3nm (30%), 5nm (33%), and 7nm (11%), technologies at 7nm and below represented 77% of total wafer revenue during the quarter.  The company also noted that, as 2nm remains in the capacity ramp-up phase, inventory days increased by seven days QoQ to 87 days.  Cost Optimization Offsets Overseas Expansion Pressure TSMC attributed its 1.5 percentage-point QoQ improvement in gross margin to 67.7% primarily to internal cost optimization and higher capacity utilization. These gains were partially offset by the margin dilution associated with the ongoing expansion of overseas fabs. Capital expenditures totaled $15.7B during the quarter.  North America Remains TSMCs Largest Market From an end-market perspective, HPC remained the primary growth driver, contributing 66% of quarterly revenue and growing 20% QoQ. Geographically, customers in North America accounted for 78% of total revenue, maintaining the region's position as TSMC's largest market.  📊 More semiconductor insights: https://buff.ly/3LTbtDT 📊 More TSMC financial charts: https://buff.ly/4cYZj3N

Micron Posts Record Revenue of $41.5B, Igniting the AI Memory Supercycle; Next Quarter Expected to Approach $50B!  Memory manufacturer Micron delivered record fiscal 3Q26 revenue of $41.46B, up 74% QoQ, driven by structural AI-related memory demand. The company also provided a strong outlook for the next quarter, forecasting revenue of approximately $50B.  DRAM and NAND Benefit from Simultaneous Volume and Pricing Growth; Data Center Revenue Exceeds $25B  By product segment, revenue growth in both DRAM and NAND was primarily driven by significant increases in ASPs. DRAM revenue reached $31.33B, accounting for 76% of total revenue, up 67% QoQ and 343% YoY. Bit shipments increased by a low-single-digit percentage sequentially, while ASPs rose by more than 60% QoQ.  NAND revenue totaled $9.94B, representing 24% of company revenue, up 99% QoQ and 361% YoY. NAND bit shipments grew by a mid-single-digit percentage sequentially, while ASPs surged by more than 80% QoQ, marking the strongest increase among Micron's major product lines.  Fueled by AI demand, the data center segment remained the primary growth driver, generating more than $25B in quarterly revenue, equivalent to an annualized run rate exceeding $100B. Data center SSD revenue surpassed $5B and more than doubled sequentially.  Chairman, President, and CEO Sanjay Mehrotra stated, "Micron's record fiscal third-quarter results and even stronger fourth-quarter outlook underscore the strategic value of memory in the AI era."  He further noted that Micron is investing at record levels in technology, products, and supply capacity to support rapidly growing customer demand. During the quarter, the company signed 16 Strategic Capacity Agreements (SCAs), covering approximately 20% of its DRAM shipments and one-third of its NAND shipments over the contract periods.  Among these agreements, 14 carry minimum-price commitments that collectively represent approximately $100B in contracted revenue. Micron also expects to receive roughly $22B in customer deposits and related financial commitments tied to the signed SCAs. Upon completion of the current agreements, approximately half or more of the company's revenue is expected to be covered by SCAs.  From an industry perspective, AI development is shifting from large-scale model training toward inference-centric Agentic AI applications, driving a structural expansion in memory demand. Meanwhile, supply constraints are unlikely to be resolved in the near term, continuing to support upward pricing momentum.  As a result, TrendForce has substantially revised upward its global memory market forecasts. The 2026 memory market value estimate has been raised from $551.6B to $889.3B. For 2027, the forecast has been increased from $842.7B to more than $1.28T, representing annual growth of approximately 44%.  📊 More semiconductor news: https://buff.ly/4466Utg 📊 More industry charts: https://buff.ly/4hbxzLr

【DDR3 Spot Price Strengthens While DDR4 Faces Constraints and Spec Downgrading】  The DRAM spot market has generally maintained its upward trajectory this week, although mainstream DDR4 chips largely traded sideways. In the DDR4 and DDR5 segments, actual transaction volumes remained limited. Despite active inquiries from buyers, suppliers have been reluctant to sell and are holding back for higher prices. This is partially due to the limited availability and high cost of DDR4 chips, which have forced some consumer DRAM buyers to downgrade their product specifications and shift toward mature products so as to secure relatively adequate supply. With HBM capacity crowding out conventional memory supply and industry-wide price increases underway, all three companies expect supply tightness to persist into 2026 and are stepping up capital investment in leading-edge processes, positioning memory as a core strategic asset in the AI era.   hashtag #SamsungElectronics reported its earnings on April 30, with its memory division posting first-quarter revenue of ₩74.8T (~$50.86B), surging 292% YoY and setting a new quarterly record.  The sharp profit increase was primarily driven by AI-related demand, with Samsung taking the lead in mass production of HBM4 and SOCAMM2 products for NVIDIA’s Vera Rubin platform, alongside rising ASPs. Looking ahead, Samsung expects server memory demand to remain strong in 2H26 and plans to begin sampling HBM4E in 2Q to reinforce its technology leadership.   hashtag #SKhynix announced its results on April 23, reporting consolidated revenue of ₩52.58T (~$35.75B) for the first quarter, with an operating margin of 72%—a new industry benchmark.  The company noted that despite the typical seasonal slowdown in 1Q, strong contributions from HBM, high-capacity server DRAM modules, and enterprise SSDs nearly doubled profits sequentially. In response to long-term structural growth, SK hynix plans to significantly increase investment this year, focusing on infrastructure for the Yongin cluster, expansion of the M15X fab, and the introduction of key equipment such as EUV lithography systems.   hashtag #MicronTechnology reported its fiscal 2Q26 results on March 18, with revenue reaching $23.86B, up 196% YoY from $8.05B.  Micron has already begun mass production of HBM4 36GB 12-high (12H) products for the Vera Rubin platform and expects revenue to climb further to a record $33.5B in fiscal 3Q26. The AI infrastructure buildout is often framed around chips and compute. But a lesser-known material is now a key factor in rising costs and lead times across the AI server supply chain: glass fiber cloth.  On the demand side, the Nvidia Rubin generation is adding pressure from multiple directions: larger substrate areas, higher layer counts, cableless designs requiring midplanes and orthogonal backplanes, and new inference-oriented rack configurations.  On the supply side, however, the market faces severe constraints. Japan's Nittobo controls roughly 90% of global T-glass supply and 60–70% of NER-glass. The company has announced capacity expansion plans, but meaningful supply relief is unlikely before mid-2027. Samsung Posts Record 1Q26 Project, Up 756% YoY, as AI Memory Drives DS Margin to 66%  Samsung Electronics reported consolidated revenue of ₩133.9T in 1Q26, up 43% QoQ and 69% YoY. Operating profit surged to ₩57.2T, rising 185% QoQ and 756% YoY, both setting new record highs, with the operating margin reaching 42.8%. The company attributed the performance to AI-driven innovation and a focus on high-value products, with the Device Solutions (DS) division serving as the primary profit engine, delivering ₩81.7T in revenue and ₩53.7T in operating profit, equivalent to a 66% margin.  The DS division recorded ₩81.7T in revenue, up 86% QoQ and 225% YoY. Within this, the memory business surged to ₩74.8T, increasing 101% QoQ and 292% YoY. Strong demand from expanding AI infrastructure and rising industry ASPs supported growth. Samsung also led the industry in mass-producing HBM4 and SOCAMM2, secured orders for NVIDIA’s Vera Rubin platform, and launched PCIe Gen6 SSDs, reinforcing its leadership in AI memory.  The foundry business faced short-term pressure from seasonality and product mix transitions, but continued to build momentum in both orders and technology. Samsung noted ongoing design activity in the HPC segment and confirmed that its silicon photonics mass production platform is now in place.   Looking ahead to 2Q26, advanced nodes are expected to maintain high utilization, supported by increased internal supply of HBM4 B-die, which should enhance profitability. Development of the 1.4nm node remains on track, while 2nm capacity is expanding with major strategic customers.  Revenue from the Device Experience (DX) division reached ₩52.7T, up 2% YoY. Meanwhile, the Digital Appliances (DA) segment is set to enter the AI data center HVAC market in 2H26, targeting growing demand for liquid cooling and data center climate control driven by rising GPU power consumption.   Although profitability improvement was limited this quarter due to cost pressures and tariffs, Samsung has clearly positioned “AI data center HVAC + premium appliance upgrades” as its next dual-engine growth strategy—signaling that the AI infrastructure theme is expanding from semiconductors into end-market hardware.  📊 More semiconductor insights: https://buff.ly/UWCDvd1 📊 More industry charts: https://buff.ly/4hbxzLr
